triage_context.environment: Library/SDK (caller is the trust boundary). Operator/build config trusted; data, object keys, and strings the caller may forward from untrusted sources treated as untrusted.
threat_model: Code execution via _.template (T1, risk-accepted by design)
triage_context.note: Subagent verifier fan-out was unavailable for this run (Claude Fable 5 not available for spawning). To keep the benchmark on-model, the 3a adversarial verification was performed inline by the Fable orchestrator rather than by independent fresh-context subagents. Verifier independence (the guard against inherited framing) is therefore reduced; votes_per_finding reflects the single orchestrator judgment.

findings.title: Prototype injection via _.extend / _.defaults copying a __proto__ own key
preconditions: Application calls _.extend or _.defaults with an attacker-controlled object as a SOURCE argument (e.g., a parsed untrusted JSON body)
The untrusted object carries a '__proto__' own-enumerable key (survives JSON.parse as an own data property)
Application later reads an inherited property off the destination object that the injected prototype now supplies
findings.rationale: Mechanism confirmed: allKeys uses `for (var key in obj)` (allKeys.js:9), so a '__proto__' own key from JSON.parse IS enumerated, and createAssigner runs obj['__proto__']=source['__proto__'] (_createAssigner.js:13). Because the destination object has no own '__proto__' data property, the bracket assignment invokes the inherited __proto__ setter and reassigns the DESTINATION object's prototype to the attacker's object. However the scanner's claimed impact ('mutating Object.prototype', 'every object in the realm inherits') is OVERSTATED: the copy is shallow, so only the target object's prototype is reassigned — this is localized prototype injection, not global Object.prototype pollution. Real and reachable when an app feeds untrusted JSON directly into extend/defaults; severity corrected from HIGH to MEDIUM and the global-pollution claim corrected. needs_manual_test because exploitability hinges on the specific embedder usage and the consumer reading an inherited flag.
findings.owner_hint: component: modules/; no CODEOWNERS; sole recent committer on _createAssigner.js is Julian Gonggrijp

findings.title: _.template compiles caller-supplied template text to executable code via new Function
findings.rationale: Confirmed that new Function(argument,'_',source) (template.js:87) compiles the template body, and <% %>/<%= %> blocks are concatenated verbatim. This is intended, documented design: SECURITY.md states 'template allows the user to inject arbitrary JavaScript code in the template string. This is allowed by design. In fact, it is the main feature' and likens the contract to eval/Function. Exclusion rule 3 (intended/documented design): the only fix is removing the feature, and the library explicitly places the trust boundary on the caller. Captured in the threat model as T1 (risk_accepted), so it is not dropped silently — but it is not an actionable library defect for triage. (The genuinely-fixable variant, CVE-2021-23358 via settings.variable, is already mitigated by the bareIdentifier check at template.js:71-74.)

findings.title: Prototype pollution in _.invert when an attacker-controlled value equals __proto__
findings.rationale: Misread of JS semantics. invert.js:8 does result[obj[_keys[i]]] = _keys[i]; the value assigned (_keys[i]) is always a STRING (a key name). When the computed key is '__proto__', `result['__proto__'] = '<string>'` invokes the __proto__ setter with a non-object, which the setter IGNORES (the prototype is only changed for object/null values). No prototype mutation occurs and no own '__proto__' property is created. The trigger as described is implausible. Exclusion: misread_code / implausible_trigger.

findings.title: Unescaped interpolation delimiter <%= %> emits raw HTML
findings.rationale: Confirmed the interpolate branch (template.js:59) emits values raw while the escape branch (line 57) routes through _.escape. Underscore is not an auto-escaping framework, so rule 14 does not auto-apply; however, raw vs escaped is selected by the template AUTHOR via the delimiter (<%= %> raw, <%- %> escaped), and template authors are necessarily trusted (see f002). Choosing the raw delimiter for untrusted data is documented, author-controlled behavior, not a library defect — exclusion rule 3 (intended design). The downstream-XSS risk is captured as threat T4 for embedder guidance, but there is no actionable library bug to fix here.

findings.title: _.result walks attacker-controlled path including __proto__/constructor
findings.rationale: result.js:14 reads obj[path[i]] and invokes function values (line 19), but this is a read/traverse primitive with no write or assignment to attacker-controlled locations. Reaching the Function constructor for code execution would require an attacker-controlled path AND a usable gadget AND a sink that does something with the returned value — no concrete exploit path is demonstrated. Exclusion rule 13 (missing-hardening / no concrete exploit) and not_actionable.

findings.title: _.escape does not encode the forward slash
findings.rationale: Confirmed the escape map (_escapeMap.js) covers & < > " ' and backtick but not '/'. Not encoding '/' is consistent with most HTML-encoding libraries and is safe for HTML body and quoted-attribute contexts; '<' and '>' are encoded, which prevents tag/closing-tag injection. No concrete exploit exists against standard contexts — defense-in-depth only. Exclusion rule 13.
